texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Motivated by practical applications where stable long-term performance is critical-such as robotics, operations research, and healthcare-we study the problem of distributionally robust (DR) average-reward reinforcement learning. We propose two algorithms that achieve near-optimal sample complexity. The first reduces the problem to a DR discounted Markov decision process (MDP), while the second, Anchored DR Average-Reward MDP, introduces an anchoring state to stabilize the controlled transition kernels within the uncertainty set. Assuming the nominal MDP is uniformly ergodic, we prove that both algorithms attain a sample complexity of Лң O ( | S || A | t 2 mix Оө -2 ) for estimating the optimal policy as well as the robust average reward under KL and f k -divergence-based uncertainty sets, provided the uncertainty radius is sufficiently small. Here, Оө is the target accuracy, | S | and | A | denote the sizes of the state and action spaces, and t mix is the mixing time of the nominal MDP. This represents the first finite-sample convergence guarantee for DR average-reward reinforcement learning. We further validate the convergence rates of our algorithms through numerical experiments.
texts.text: Motivated by practical applications where stable long-term performance is critical-such as robotics, operations research, and healthcare-we study the problem of distributionally robust (DR) average-reward reinforcement learning. We propose two algorithms that achieve near-optimal sample complexity. The first reduces the problem to a DR discounted Markov decision process (MDP), while the second, Anchored DR Average-Reward MDP, introduces an anchoring state to stabilize the controlled transition kernels within the uncertainty set. Assuming the nominal MDP is uniformly ergodic, we prove that both algorithms attain a sample complexity of Лң O ( | S || A | t 2 mix Оө -2 ) for estimating the optimal policy as well as the robust average reward under KL and f k -divergence-based uncertainty sets, provided the uncertainty radius is sufficiently small. Here, Оө is the target accuracy, | S | and | A | denote the sizes of the state and action spaces, and t mix is the mixing time of the nominal MDP. This represents the first finite-sample convergence guarantee for DR average-reward reinforcement learning. We further validate the convergence rates of our algorithms through numerical experiments.

texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Reinforcement learning (RL) [35] is a core machine learning framework in which agents learn to make decisions by interacting with their environments to maximize long-term rewards. RL has been successfully applied across a wide range of domains-from classic applications in robotics and control systems [19, 13] to more recent advances in game playing [21, 6, 8] and large language model (LLM)-driven reasoning tasks [47, 14].
texts.text: Reinforcement learning (RL) [35] is a core machine learning framework in which agents learn to make decisions by interacting with their environments to maximize long-term rewards. RL has been successfully applied across a wide range of domains-from classic applications in robotics and control systems [19, 13] to more recent advances in game playing [21, 6, 8] and large language model (LLM)-driven reasoning tasks [47, 14].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A central assumption in RL is that the training environment (e.g. a simulator) faithfully represents the real-world deployment setting. In practice, however, this assumption rarely holds, leading to fragile policies underperform when exposed to mismatches between training and deployment environments. This remains a major obstacle to translating RL's successes in simulated settings to reliable performance in real-world applications.
texts.text: A central assumption in RL is that the training environment (e.g. a simulator) faithfully represents the real-world deployment setting. In practice, however, this assumption rarely holds, leading to fragile policies underperform when exposed to mismatches between training and deployment environments. This remains a major obstacle to translating RL's successes in simulated settings to reliable performance in real-world applications.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Average-reward RL is not only important but also more challenging in terms of algorithm design and theoretical analysis. In the standard (non-robust) RL setting, the minimax sample complexity for generative models in discounted-reward cases was resolved as early as 2013 [1]. In contrast, analogous results for average-reward settings were only developed much later, with recent advances in Wang et al. [37], Zurek and Chen [57, 58, 59, 60] under granular structural assumptions.
texts.text: Average-reward RL is not only important but also more challenging in terms of algorithm design and theoretical analysis. In the standard (non-robust) RL setting, the minimax sample complexity for generative models in discounted-reward cases was resolved as early as 2013 [1]. In contrast, analogous results for average-reward settings were only developed much later, with recent advances in Wang et al. [37], Zurek and Chen [57, 58, 59, 60] under granular structural assumptions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper marks the first systematic analysis of the statistical properties of distributional robust average-reward MDPs (DR-AMDPs) in the tabular setting, addressing a critical gap in the literature.
texts.text: This paper marks the first systematic analysis of the statistical properties of distributional robust average-reward MDPs (DR-AMDPs) in the tabular setting, addressing a critical gap in the literature.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Specifically, we propose two algorithms that achieve near-optimal (in a minmax sense) sample complexity for learning DR-AMDPs. The first is based on a reduction to the DR discounted-reward MDP (DR-DMDP), where a discount factor Оі must be carefully chosen to balance the trade-off between finite sample statistical error and the algorithmic bias introduced by the reduction. The second algorithm, anchored DR-AMDP, modifies the entire uncertainty set of transition kernels by introducing an anchoring state with a certain calibration probability.
texts.text: Specifically, we propose two algorithms that achieve near-optimal (in a minmax sense) sample complexity for learning DR-AMDPs. The first is based on a reduction to the DR discounted-reward MDP (DR-DMDP), where a discount factor Оі must be carefully chosen to balance the trade-off between finite sample statistical error and the algorithmic bias introduced by the reduction. The second algorithm, anchored DR-AMDP, modifies the entire uncertainty set of transition kernels by introducing an anchoring state with a certain calibration probability.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To demonstrate their statistical efficiency, we consider a tabular setting where the nominal MDP is uniformly ergodic (Definition 3.3) with a uniform mixing time upper bound t mix for all stationary, Markovian, and deterministic policies. We show that, to learn the optimal robust average reward and policy within Оө accuracy, both algorithms achieve a sample complexity of Лң O ( | S || A | t 2 mix Оө -2 ) under KL and f k -divergence uncertainty sets, assuming a sufficiently small uncertainty radius Оҙ (to be defined). Here, | S | and | A | denote the cardinality of the state and action spaces, respectively. Compared to standard (non-robust) average-reward RL literature, this rate is optimal in its dependence on | S || A | and Оө .
texts.text: To demonstrate their statistical efficiency, we consider a tabular setting where the nominal MDP is uniformly ergodic (Definition 3.3) with a uniform mixing time upper bound t mix for all stationary, Markovian, and deterministic policies. We show that, to learn the optimal robust average reward and policy within Оө accuracy, both algorithms achieve a sample complexity of Лң O ( | S || A | t 2 mix Оө -2 ) under KL and f k -divergence uncertainty sets, assuming a sufficiently small uncertainty radius Оҙ (to be defined). Here, | S | and | A | denote the cardinality of the state and action spaces, respectively. Compared to standard (non-robust) average-reward RL literature, this rate is optimal in its dependence on | S || A | and Оө .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our analysis establishes three key contributions to the theory of DR-RL under the average-reward criterion. First, we address a fundamental modeling challenge: conventional uncertainty sets can contain MDPs that are not unichain, thereby invalidating the standard Bellman equations. To resolve this issue, we derive structural conditions on the uncertainty set that ensure stability for all MDPs within it. Second, we develop and analyze the reduction yielding the first stability-sensitive sample complexity bound for DR-DMDPs of Лң O ( | S || A | t 2 mix (1 -Оі ) -2 Оө -2 ) and hence the aforementioned upper bound for DR-AMDPs. Building on this framework, we introduce the anchored algorithm and show that its output coincides with that of the reduction approach under a suitable choice of the anchoring parameter. Third, both algorithms are designed to function without requiring prior knowledge of model-specific parameters, particularly the mixing time t mix . Collectively, our work offers a unified treatment that connects robustness, stability, algorithm design, and finite-sample guarantees for DR-RL under the average-reward criterion.
texts.text: Our analysis establishes three key contributions to the theory of DR-RL under the average-reward criterion. First, we address a fundamental modeling challenge: conventional uncertainty sets can contain MDPs that are not unichain, thereby invalidating the standard Bellman equations. To resolve this issue, we derive structural conditions on the uncertainty set that ensure stability for all MDPs within it. Second, we develop and analyze the reduction yielding the first stability-sensitive sample complexity bound for DR-DMDPs of Лң O ( | S || A | t 2 mix (1 -Оі ) -2 Оө -2 ) and hence the aforementioned upper bound for DR-AMDPs. Building on this framework, we introduce the anchored algorithm and show that its output coincides with that of the reduction approach under a suitable choice of the anchoring parameter. Third, both algorithms are designed to function without requiring prior knowledge of model-specific parameters, particularly the mixing time t mix . Collectively, our work offers a unified treatment that connects robustness, stability, algorithm design, and finite-sample guarantees for DR-RL under the average-reward criterion.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: Section 2 surveys existing results for both standard and robust RLs. Section 3 introduces the mathematical preliminaries, including key notations and problem formulation. Our main theoretical contributions, including algorithmic development and sample complexity analysis, are presented in Section 4. Finally, Section 5 provides empirical validation of our theoretical findings and Section 6 concludes the paper and discusses future work.
texts.text: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: Section 2 surveys existing results for both standard and robust RLs. Section 3 introduces the mathematical preliminaries, including key notations and problem formulation. Our main theoretical contributions, including algorithmic development and sample complexity analysis, are presented in Section 4. Finally, Section 5 provides empirical validation of our theoretical findings and Section 6 concludes the paper and discusses future work.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2 Literature Review
texts.text: 2 Literature Review

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sample Complexity of Discounted-Reward Tabular RL: There is an extensive literature on the sample complexity of tabular reinforcement learning. In standard (non-robust) settings, the minimax sample complexity for discounted-reward problems has been well studied. Early works by Azar et al. [1], Li et al. [20] established the minimax rate of Лң Оҳ( | S || A | (1 -Оі ) -3 Оө -2 ) . More recent research has shifted toward instance-dependent bounds that leverage structural properties of the MDP. For example, Wang et al. [38], Zurek and Chen [59] derive tighter instance-dependent bounds of Лң O ( | S || A | t mix (1 -Оі ) -2 Оө -2 ) and Лң O ( | S || A | H(1 -Оі ) -2 Оө -2 ) under the assumptions that P is uniformly ergodic or weakly communicating, where H denotes the span of the relative value function.
texts.text: Sample Complexity of Discounted-Reward Tabular RL: There is an extensive literature on the sample complexity of tabular reinforcement learning. In standard (non-robust) settings, the minimax sample complexity for discounted-reward problems has been well studied. Early works by Azar et al. [1], Li et al. [20] established the minimax rate of Лң Оҳ( | S || A | (1 -Оі ) -3 Оө -2 ) . More recent research has shifted toward instance-dependent bounds that leverage structural properties of the MDP. For example, Wang et al. [38], Zurek and Chen [59] derive tighter instance-dependent bounds of Лң O ( | S || A | t mix (1 -Оі ) -2 Оө -2 ) and Лң O ( | S || A | H(1 -Оі ) -2 Оө -2 ) under the assumptions that P is uniformly ergodic or weakly communicating, where H denotes the span of the relative value function.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sample Complexity of Average-Reward Tabular RL: Recently, there has been growing interest in the sample complexity of average-reward reinforcement learning in standard (non-robust) settings. Early work by Jin and Sidford [17] established a bound of Лң O ( | S || A | t 2 mix Оө -2 ) using primal-dual stochastic mirror descent. A rate of Лң O ( | S || A | t mix Оө -3 ) was established later via a reduction to the discounted MDP setting [18]. Subsequent analyses achieved tighter bounds: Wang et al. [38] obtained a rate of Лң O ( | S || A | t mix Оө -2 ) , while Zurek and Chen [57, 59] derived instance-dependent optimal rates of Лң O ( | S || A | H Оө -2 ) for weakly communicating MDPs. Further refinements [58, 60] achieved similar rates without requiring prior knowledge, using plug-in and span penalization approaches. Beyond the generative model setting, Zhang et al. [55] and Chen [5] provided finite-sample analyses for synchronous and asynchronous Q-learning, respectively. Asymptotic properties were also studied in Yu et al. [54], Wan et al. [36] for asynchronous Q -learning.
texts.text: Sample Complexity of Average-Reward Tabular RL: Recently, there has been growing interest in the sample complexity of average-reward reinforcement learning in standard (non-robust) settings. Early work by Jin and Sidford [17] established a bound of Лң O ( | S || A | t 2 mix Оө -2 ) using primal-dual stochastic mirror descent. A rate of Лң O ( | S || A | t mix Оө -3 ) was established later via a reduction to the discounted MDP setting [18]. Subsequent analyses achieved tighter bounds: Wang et al. [38] obtained a rate of Лң O ( | S || A | t mix Оө -2 ) , while Zurek and Chen [57, 59] derived instance-dependent optimal rates of Лң O ( | S || A | H Оө -2 ) for weakly communicating MDPs. Further refinements [58, 60] achieved similar rates without requiring prior knowledge, using plug-in and span penalization approaches. Beyond the generative model setting, Zhang et al. [55] and Chen [5] provided finite-sample analyses for synchronous and asynchronous Q-learning, respectively. Asymptotic properties were also studied in Yu et al. [54], Wan et al. [36] for asynchronous Q -learning.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: DR-DMDP and DR-RL: Our work builds on the theoretical foundations of robust MDPs [11, 16, 27, 48, 49, 31, 42], which primarily develop dynamic programming principles under the discountedreward setting. Recent advances in distributionally robust reinforcement learning (DR-RL) have investigated the sample complexity of DR-DMDPs under various divergence-based uncertainty sets. For example, Wang et al. [43], Shi and Chi [32] establish a model-free upper bound of Лң O ( | S || A | (1 -Оі ) -4 Оө -2 ) under KL-divergence. Under ПҮ 2 -divergence, Shi et al. [33] obtain a similar upper bound, while Clavier et al. [7] show that l p -norm constraints admit a tighter minimax rate of Лң Оҳ( | S || A | (1 -Оі ) -3 Оө -2 ) . In this paper, by incorporating the mixing time parameter, we present a 'instance-dependence" sample complexity bound of Лң O ( | S || A | t 2 mix (1 -Оі ) -2 Оө -2 ) , which improves the dependence on the effective horizon from (1 -Оі ) -4 to (1 -Оі ) -2 . Several other works contribute to the theoretical and algorithmic landscape of DR-RL in various settings, including Panaganti and Kalathil [28], Yang et al. [52], Xu et al. [50], Blanchet et al. [3], Liu et al. [23], Wang et al. [41], Yang et al. [51].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We briefly review and define some notations for classical tabular MDP models. Let вҲҶ( S ) denotes the probability simplex over R S . Afinite discounted MDP (DMDP) is defined by the tuple ( S , A , r, P, Оі ) . Here, S , A denote the finite state and action spaces respectively; r : S Г— A вҶ’ [0 , 1] is the reward function; P = { p s,a вҲҲ вҲҶ( S ) : ( s, a ) вҲҲ S Г— A } is the controlled transition kernel, and Оі вҲҲ (0 , 1) is the discount factor. An average-reward MDP (AMDP) model, on the other hand, is specified by ( S , A , r, P ) without the discount factor.

texts.orig: Discounted-reward MDP (DMDP): Given a DMDP instance ( S , A , r, P, Оі ) and ПҖ вҲҲ О  , the discounted value function V ПҖ P : S вҶ’ R is defined as: V ПҖ P ( s ) = E ПҖ P [ вҲ‘ вҲһ t =0 Оі t r ( S t , A t ) | S 0 = s ] . An optimal policy ПҖ вҲ— вҲҲ О  achieves the optimal value V вҲ— P ( s ) := max ПҖ вҲҲ О  V ПҖ P ( s ) .

An optimal policy ПҖ вҲ— вҲҲ О  achieves the optimal value V вҲ— P ( s ) := max ПҖ вҲҲ О  V ПҖ P ( s ) .

texts.text: Average-reward MDP (AMDP): For AMDP model ( S , A , r, P ) and ПҖ вҲҲ О  , the long-run averagereward function g ПҖ P : S вҶ’ R is defined as g ПҖ P ( s ) := lim sup T вҶ’вҲһ T -1 E ПҖ P [ вҲ‘ T -1 t =0 r ( S t , A t ) | S 0 = s ] .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2 Uniform Ergodicity
texts.text: 3.2 Uniform Ergodicity

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Motivated by engineering applications where policies induce systems that are stable in the long run, we consider a stability property of MDPs known as uniform ergodicity , a stronger version of the unichain property. In this setting, the controlled Markov chain induced by any reasonable policy converges in distribution to a unique steady state in total variation distance вҲҘВ·вҲҘ TV defined by вҲҘ p -q вҲҘ TV := sup A вҠӮ S | p ( A ) -q ( A ) | for probability vectors p, q вҲҲ вҲҶ( S ) .
texts.text: Motivated by engineering applications where policies induce systems that are stable in the long run, we consider a stability property of MDPs known as uniform ergodicity , a stronger version of the unichain property. In this setting, the controlled Markov chain induced by any reasonable policy converges in distribution to a unique steady state in total variation distance вҲҘВ·вҲҘ TV defined by вҲҘ p -q вҲҘ TV := sup A вҠӮ S | p ( A ) -q ( A ) | for probability vectors p, q вҲҲ вҲҶ( S ) .

(Mixing Time and Minorization Time) Define the mixing time of a uniformly ergodic transition kernel P as t mix ( K ) := inf { m вүҘ 1 : max s вҲҲ S вҲҘ K m ( s, В· ) -ПҒ ( В· ) вҲҘ TV вүӨ 1 4 } , and the minorization time as t minorize ( K ) := inf { m/p : min s вҲҲ S K m ( s, В· ) вүҘ pПҲ ( В· ) for some ПҲ вҲҲ вҲҶ( S ) } .
texts.text: Definition 3.2. (Mixing Time and Minorization Time) Define the mixing time of a uniformly ergodic transition kernel P as t mix ( K ) := inf { m вүҘ 1 : max s вҲҲ S вҲҘ K m ( s, В· ) -ПҒ ( В· ) вҲҘ TV вүӨ 1 4 } , and the minorization time as t minorize ( K ) := inf { m/p : min s вҲҲ S K m ( s, В· ) вүҘ pПҲ ( В· ) for some ПҲ вҲҲ вҲҶ( S ) } .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is shown in Theorem 1 of Wang et al. [37] that for a uniformly ergodic transition kernel K , these metrics of stability are equivalent up to constants: t minorize ( K ) вүӨ 22 t mix ( K ) вүӨ 22 log(16) t minorize ( K ) .
texts.text: It is shown in Theorem 1 of Wang et al. [37] that for a uniformly ergodic transition kernel K , these metrics of stability are equivalent up to constants: t minorize ( K ) вүӨ 22 t mix ( K ) вүӨ 22 log(16) t minorize ( K ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While the MDP sample complexity literature typically uses the mixing time as a complexity parameter, for our purposes, the Doeblin condition and the associated minorization time offer sharper theoretical insights into how adversarial robustness affects the statistical complexity of RL. Given the equivalence between t mix ( K ) and t minorize ( K ) , and the latter's advantage in revealing these insights, we will use t minorize ( K ) time throughout this work.
texts.text: While the MDP sample complexity literature typically uses the mixing time as a complexity parameter, for our purposes, the Doeblin condition and the associated minorization time offer sharper theoretical insights into how adversarial robustness affects the statistical complexity of RL. Given the equivalence between t mix ( K ) and t minorize ( K ) , and the latter's advantage in revealing these insights, we will use t minorize ( K ) time throughout this work.

texts.orig: 3.3 Distributionally Robust Discounted-Reward and Average-Reward MDPs
texts.text: 3.3 Distributionally Robust Discounted-Reward and Average-Reward MDPs

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper focuses on a robust MDP setting where the stochastic dynamics of the system is influenced by adversarial perturbations on the transition structure. We assume the presence of an adversary that can transition probabilities within KL or f k -divergence uncertainty sets. Specifically, for probability measures q, p вҲҲ вҲҶ( S ) where q is absolutely continuous w.r.t. p , denoted by q вүӘ p , we define D KL ( q || p ) := вҲ‘ s вҲҲ S log( q ( s ) /p ( s )) q ( s ) and D f k ( q || p ) := вҲ‘ s вҲҲ S f k ( q ( s ) /p ( s )) p ( s ) . Here, the function f k is defined for k вҲҲ (1 , вҲһ ) by f k ( t ) = ( t k -kt + k -1) / ( k ( k -1)) . When k = 2 , D f k is the ПҮ 2 -divergence.
texts.text: This paper focuses on a robust MDP setting where the stochastic dynamics of the system is influenced by adversarial perturbations on the transition structure. We assume the presence of an adversary that can transition probabilities within KL or f k -divergence uncertainty sets. Specifically, for probability measures q, p вҲҲ вҲҶ( S ) where q is absolutely continuous w.r.t. p , denoted by q вүӘ p , we define D KL ( q || p ) := вҲ‘ s вҲҲ S log( q ( s ) /p ( s )) q ( s ) and D f k ( q || p ) := вҲ‘ s вҲҲ S f k ( q ( s ) /p ( s )) p ( s ) . Here, the function f k is defined for k вҲҲ (1 , вҲһ ) by f k ( t ) = ( t k -kt + k -1) / ( k ( k -1)) . When k = 2 , D f k is the ПҮ 2 -divergence.

This emphasizes the necessity of limiting the adversarial power to obtain a stable worst-case system and state-independent average reward.
texts.text: The proof is deferred to Appendix B, E. We further note that without Assumption 2, the Hard MDP instance in Section 5 will have a non-mixing worst-case adversarial kernel and state-dependent optimal average reward even when Оҙ = Оҳ( p вҲ§ /m 2 вҲЁ ) . This emphasizes the necessity of limiting the adversarial power to obtain a stable worst-case system and state-independent average reward.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We propose two algorithms: Reduction to DR-DMDP and Anchored DR-AMDP. Notably, these are the first to provide finite-sample guarantees for DR-AMDPs and achieve the canonical n -1 / 2 convergence rate in policy and estimation. Furthermore, both algorithms operate without requiring prior knowledge of t minorize . Together, these contributions represent foundational advances in the study of data-driven learning of DR-AMDPs.
texts.text: We propose two algorithms: Reduction to DR-DMDP and Anchored DR-AMDP. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1 Reduction to DR-DMDP
texts.text: 4.1 Reduction to DR-DMDP

texts.label: text
texts.orig: First, we present the algorithmic reduction from DR-AMDP to DR-DMDP. The algorithm design in this section is inspired by prior works [46, 18]. Specifically, we apply value iteration to an auxiliary empirical DR-DMDP model to obtain both the value function and optimal policy. Utilizing a calibrated discount Оі = 1 -n -1 / 2 where n is the input sample size in Algorithm 1, we achieve an Оө -approximation of the target DR-AMDP value and policy with the auxiliary DR-DMDP using Лң O ( | S || A | t 2 minorize p -1 вҲ§ Оө -2 ) samples.
texts.text: First, we present the algorithmic reduction from DR-AMDP to DR-DMDP. The algorithm design in this section is inspired by prior works [46, 18]. Specifically, we apply value iteration to an auxiliary empirical DR-DMDP model to obtain both the value function and optimal policy. Utilizing a calibrated discount Оі = 1 -n -1 / 2 where n is the input sample size in Algorithm 1, we achieve an Оө -approximation of the target DR-AMDP value and policy with the auxiliary DR-DMDP using Лң O ( | S || A | t 2 minorize p -1 вҲ§ Оө -2 ) samples.

We note that Theorem 4.3 implies that to achieve an Оө -optimal policy as well as producing a uniform Оө -error estimate of V вҲ— P with high probability using Algorithm 1, we need Лң O ( | S || A | t 2 minorize (1 -Оі ) -2 p -1 вҲ§ Оө -2 ) samples. Compared to state-of-the-art sample complexity results for DR-DMDPs [32, 43, 33], Theorem 4.3 provides a significant refinement: when the nominal controlled kernel is uniformly ergodic, the effective horizon dependence improves to (1 -Оі ) -2 . Notably, this (1 -Оі ) -2 scaling is also known to be optimal in the non-robust setting [37], which corresponds to DR-DMDPs when Оҙ = 0 . As we will show, this optimal dependence directly enables the canonical n -1 / 2 convergence rate for policy learning and value estimation in the DR-AMDP setting.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2 Anchored DR-AMDP
texts.text: 4.2 Anchored DR-AMDP

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we develop anchored DR-AMDP Algorithm 3 that avoids solving a DR-DMDP subproblem. Inspired by Fruit et al. [10], Zurek and Chen [58]'s anchoring approach for classical MDPs, our anchored DR-AMDP approach modifies the entire uncertainty set of controlled transition kernels via a uniform anchoring state s 0 and a calibrated strength parameter Оҫ . We show that Algorithm 3 enjoys the same error and sample complexity upper bounds to Algorithm 2.
texts.text: In this section, we develop anchored DR-AMDP Algorithm 3 that avoids solving a DR-DMDP subproblem. Inspired by Fruit et al. [10], Zurek and Chen [58]'s anchoring approach for classical MDPs, our anchored DR-AMDP approach modifies the entire uncertainty set of controlled transition kernels via a uniform anchoring state s 0 and a calibrated strength parameter Оҫ . We show that Algorithm 3 enjoys the same error and sample complexity upper bounds to Algorithm 2.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5 Numerical Experiments
texts.text: 5 Numerical Experiments

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we present numerical experiments to validate our theoretical results. We employ the Hard MDP family introduced in Wang et al. [37], which confirms a minimax sample complexity lower bound of в„Ұ( t minorize Оө -2 ) for estimating the average reward to within an Оө absolute error in the non-robust setting, matching the known upper bound. Our experiments show an empirical convergence rate of n -1 / 2 for both algorithms, validating them as the first algorithms that achieve this rate in the DR-AMDP setting.
texts.text: In this section, we present numerical experiments to validate our theoretical results. We employ the Hard MDP family introduced in Wang et al. [37], which confirms a minimax sample complexity lower bound of в„Ұ( t minorize Оө -2 ) for estimating the average reward to within an Оө absolute error in the non-robust setting, matching the known upper bound. Our experiments show an empirical convergence rate of n -1 / 2 for both algorithms, validating them as the first algorithms that achieve this rate in the DR-AMDP setting.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6 Conclusion and Future Work
texts.text: 6 Conclusion and Future Work

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, we study distributionally robust average-reward reinforcement learning under a generative model. We first establish an instance-dependent bound of Лң O ( | S || A | t 2 minorize (1 -Оі ) -2 Оө -2 ) for DR-DMDP. Building on this result, we propose two a priori knowledge-free algorithms with finitesample complexity Лң O ( | S || A | t 2 minorize Оө -2 ) . Our work provides novel insights into the relationship between uniform ergodicity and sample complexity under distributional robustness.
texts.text: In this work, we study distributionally robust average-reward reinforcement learning under a generative model. We first establish an instance-dependent bound of Лң O ( | S || A | t 2 minorize (1 -Оі ) -2 Оө -2 ) for DR-DMDP. Building on this result, we propose two a priori knowledge-free algorithms with finitesample complexity Лң O ( | S || A | t 2 minorize Оө -2 ) . Our work provides novel insights into the relationship between uniform ergodicity and sample complexity under distributional robustness.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While our results rely on the assumptions of uniform ergodicity and constraints on the uncertainty size, we acknowledge these as potential limitations. For future work, we plan to generalize these results to weakly communicating settings and, potentially, multichain MDPs, and investigate broader uncertainty sets (e.g., l p -balls and Wasserstein metrics).
texts.text: While our results rely on the assumptions of uniform ergodicity and constraints on the uncertainty size, we acknowledge these as potential limitations. For future work, we plan to generalize these results to weakly communicating settings and, potentially, multichain MDPs, and investigate broader uncertainty sets (e.g., l p -balls and Wasserstein metrics).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: NeurIPS Paper Checklist
texts.text: NeurIPS Paper Checklist

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Claims
texts.text: 1. Claims

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Question: Do the main claims made in the abstract and introduction accurately reflect the paper's contributions and scope?
texts.text: Question: Do the main claims made in the abstract and introduction accurately reflect the paper's contributions and scope?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Answer: [Yes]
texts.text: Answer: [Yes]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Justification: All the main claims accurately reflect the paper's contribution.
texts.text: Justification: All the main claims accurately reflect the paper's contribution.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Guidelines:
texts.text: Guidelines:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· The answer NA means that the abstract and introduction do not include the claims made in the paper.
texts.text: The answer NA means that the abstract and introduction do not include the claims made in the paper.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· The abstract and/or introduction should clearly state the claims made, including the contributions made in the paper and important assumptions and limitations. A No or NA answer to this question will not be perceived well by the reviewers.
texts.text: The abstract and/or introduction should clearly state the claims made, including the contributions made in the paper and important assumptions and limitations. A No or NA answer to this question will not be perceived well by the reviewers.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· The claims made should match theoretical and experimental results, and reflect how much the results can be expected to generalize to other settings.
texts.text: The claims made should match theoretical and experimental results, and reflect how much the results can be expected to generalize to other settings.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· It is fine to include aspirational goals as motivation as long as it is clear that these goals are not attained by the paper.
texts.text: It is fine to include aspirational goals as motivation as long as it is clear that these goals are not attained by the paper.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Limitations
texts.text: 2. Limitations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Question: Does the paper discuss the limitations of the work performed by the authors?
texts.text: Question: Does the paper discuss the limitations of the work performed by the authors?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Answer: [Yes]
texts.text: Answer: [Yes]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Justification: The limitations due to assumptions made for the theoretical guarantee are discussed in Section 6.
texts.text: Justification: The limitations due to assumptions made for the theoretical guarantee are discussed in Section 6.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guidelines:
texts.text: Guidelines:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· The answer NA means that the paper has no limitation while the answer No means that the paper has limitations, but those are not discussed in the paper.
texts.text: The answer NA means that the paper has no limitation while the answer No means that the paper has limitations, but those are not discussed in the paper.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· The authors are encouraged to create a separate "Limitations" section in their paper.
texts.text: The authors are encouraged to create a separate "Limitations" section in their paper.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· The paper should point out any strong assumptions and how robust the results are to violations of these assumptions (e.g., independence assumptions, noiseless settings, model well-specification, asymptotic approximations only holding locally). The authors should reflect on how these assumptions might be violated in practice and what the implications would be.
texts.text: The paper should point out any strong assumptions and how robust the results are to violations of these assumptions (e.g., independence assumptions, noiseless settings, model well-specification, asymptotic approximations only holding locally). The authors should reflect on how these assumptions might be violated in practice and what the implications would be.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· The authors should reflect on the scope of the claims made, e.g., if the approach was only tested on a few datasets or with a few runs. In general, empirical results often depend on implicit assumptions, which should be articulated.
texts.text: The authors should reflect on the scope of the claims made, e.g., if the approach was only tested on a few datasets or with a few runs. In general, empirical results often depend on implicit assumptions, which should be articulated.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· The authors should reflect on the factors that influence the performance of the approach. For example, a facial recognition algorithm may perform poorly when image resolution is low or images are taken in low lighting. Or a speech-to-text system might not be used reliably to provide closed captions for online lectures because it fails to handle technical jargon.
texts.text: The authors should reflect on the factors that influence the performance of the approach. For example, a facial recognition algorithm may perform poorly when image resolution is low or images are taken in low lighting. Or a speech-to-text system might not be used reliably to provide closed captions for online lectures because it fails to handle technical jargon.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· The authors should discuss the computational efficiency of the proposed algorithms and how they scale with dataset size.
texts.text: The authors should discuss the computational efficiency of the proposed algorithms and how they scale with dataset size.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· If applicable, the authors should discuss possible limitations of their approach to address problems of privacy and fairness.
texts.text: If applicable, the authors should discuss possible limitations of their approach to address problems of privacy and fairness.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· While the authors might fear that complete honesty about limitations might be used by reviewers as grounds for rejection, a worse outcome might be that reviewers discover limitations that aren't acknowledged in the paper. The authors should use their best judgment and recognize that individual actions in favor of transparency play an important role in developing norms that preserve the integrity of the community. Reviewers will be specifically instructed to not penalize honesty concerning limitations.
texts.text: While the authors might fear that complete honesty about limitations might be used by reviewers as grounds for rejection, a worse outcome might be that reviewers discover limitations that aren't acknowledged in the paper. The authors should use their best judgment and recognize that individual actions in favor of transparency play an important role in developing norms that preserve the integrity of the community. Reviewers will be specifically instructed to not penalize honesty concerning limitations.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Theory assumptions and proofs
texts.text: 3. Theory assumptions and proofs

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Question: For each theoretical result, does the paper provide the full set of assumptions and a complete (and correct) proof?
texts.text: Question: For each theoretical result, does the paper provide the full set of assumptions and a complete (and correct) proof?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Answer: [Yes]
texts.text: Answer: [Yes]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Justification: The full set of assumptions is formally stated in Section 4, with rigorous proofs provided in the appendix.
texts.text: Justification: The full set of assumptions is formally stated in Section 4, with rigorous proofs provided in the appendix.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Guidelines:
texts.text: Guidelines:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· The answer NA means that the paper does not include theoretical results.
texts.text: The answer NA means that the paper does not include theoretical results.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· All the theorems, formulas, and proofs in the paper should be numbered and crossreferenced.
texts.text: All the theorems, formulas, and proofs in the paper should be numbered and crossreferenced.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· All assumptions should be clearly stated or referenced in the statement of any theorems.
texts.text: All assumptions should be clearly stated or referenced in the statement of any theorems.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· The proofs can either appear in the main paper or the supplemental material, but if they appear in the supplemental material, the authors are encouraged to provide a short proof sketch to provide intuition.
texts.text: The proofs can either appear in the main paper or the supplemental material, but if they appear in the supplemental material, the authors are encouraged to provide a short proof sketch to provide intuition.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· Inversely, any informal proof provided in the core of the paper should be complemented by formal proofs provided in appendix or supplemental material.
texts.text: Inversely, any informal proof provided in the core of the paper should be complemented by formal proofs provided in appendix or supplemental material.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· Theorems and Lemmas that the proof relies upon should be properly referenced.
texts.text: Theorems and Lemmas that the proof relies upon should be properly referenced.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Experimental result reproducibility
texts.text: 4. Experimental result reproducibility

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Question: Does the paper fully disclose all the information needed to reproduce the main experimental results of the paper to the extent that it affects the main claims and/or conclusions of the paper (regardless of whether the code and data are provided or not)?
texts.text: Question: Does the paper fully disclose all the information needed to reproduce the main experimental results of the paper to the extent that it affects the main claims and/or conclusions of the paper (regardless of whether the code and data are provided or not)?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Answer: [Yes]
texts.text: Answer: [Yes]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Justification: The complete algorithmic implementation under the generative model is provided in Section 4, including detailed pseudo-code. Section 5 specifies all experimental configurations and parameter settings, ensuring full reproducibility of our results.
texts.text: Justification: The complete algorithmic implementation under the generative model is provided in Section 4, including detailed pseudo-code. Section 5 specifies all experimental configurations and parameter settings, ensuring full reproducibility of our results.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Guidelines:
texts.text: Guidelines:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· The answer NA means that the paper does not include experiments.
texts.text: The answer NA means that the paper does not include experiments.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· If the paper includes experiments, a No answer to this question will not be perceived well by the reviewers: Making the paper reproducible is important, regardless of whether the code and data are provided or not.
texts.text: If the paper includes experiments, a No answer to this question will not be perceived well by the reviewers: Making the paper reproducible is important, regardless of whether the code and data are provided or not.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· If the contribution is a dataset and/or model, the authors should describe the steps taken to make their results reproducible or verifiable.
texts.text: If the contribution is a dataset and/or model, the authors should describe the steps taken to make their results reproducible or verifiable.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· Depending on the contribution, reproducibility can be accomplished in various ways. For example, if the contribution is a novel architecture, describing the architecture fully might suffice, or if the contribution is a specific model and empirical evaluation, it may be necessary to either make it possible for others to replicate the model with the same dataset, or provide access to the model. In general. releasing code and data is often one good way to accomplish this, but reproducibility can also be provided via detailed instructions for how to replicate the results, access to a hosted model (e.g., in the case of a large language model), releasing of a model checkpoint, or other means that are appropriate to the research performed.
texts.text: Depending on the contribution, reproducibility can be accomplished in various ways. For example, if the contribution is a novel architecture, describing the architecture fully might suffice, or if the contribution is a specific model and empirical evaluation, it may be necessary to either make it possible for others to replicate the model with the same dataset, or provide access to the model. In general. releasing code and data is often one good way to accomplish this, but reproducibility can also be provided via detailed instructions for how to replicate the results, access to a hosted model (e.g., in the case of a large language model), releasing of a model checkpoint, or other means that are appropriate to the research performed.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· While NeurIPS does not require releasing code, the conference does require all submissions to provide some reasonable avenue for reproducibility, which may depend on the nature of the contribution. For example
texts.text: While NeurIPS does not require releasing code, the conference does require all submissions to provide some reasonable avenue for reproducibility, which may depend on the nature of the contribution. For example

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (a) If the contribution is primarily a new algorithm, the paper should make it clear how to reproduce that algorithm.
texts.text: (a) If the contribution is primarily a new algorithm, the paper should make it clear how to reproduce that algorithm.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (b) If the contribution is primarily a new model architecture, the paper should describe the architecture clearly and fully.
texts.text: (b) If the contribution is primarily a new model architecture, the paper should describe the architecture clearly and fully.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (c) If the contribution is a new model (e.g., a large language model), then there should either be a way to access this model for reproducing the results or a way to reproduce the model (e.g., with an open-source dataset or instructions for how to construct the dataset).
texts.text: (c) If the contribution is a new model (e.g., a large language model), then there should either be a way to access this model for reproducing the results or a way to reproduce the model (e.g., with an open-source dataset or instructions for how to construct the dataset).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (d) We recognize that reproducibility may be tricky in some cases, in which case authors are welcome to describe the particular way they provide for reproducibility. In the case of closed-source models, it may be that access to the model is limited in some way (e.g., to registered users), but it should be possible for other researchers to have some path to reproducing or verifying the results.
texts.text: (d) We recognize that reproducibility may be tricky in some cases, in which case authors are welcome to describe the particular way they provide for reproducibility. In the case of closed-source models, it may be that access to the model is limited in some way (e.g., to registered users), but it should be possible for other researchers to have some path to reproducing or verifying the results.

texts.text: 5. Open access to data and code

texts.text: Question: Does the paper provide open access to the data and code, with sufficient instructions to faithfully reproduce the main experimental results, as described in supplemental material?

texts.text: Answer: [Yes]

texts.label: list_item

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· Please see the NeurIPS code and data submission guidelines ( https://nips.cc/ public/guides/CodeSubmissionPolicy ) for more details.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· While we encourage the release of code and data, we understand that this might not be possible, so 'No' is an acceptable answer. Papers cannot be rejected simply for not including code, unless this is central to the contribution (e.g., for a new open-source benchmark).
texts.text: While we encourage the release of code and data, we understand that this might not be possible, so 'No' is an acceptable answer. Papers cannot be rejected simply for not including code, unless this is central to the contribution (e.g., for a new open-source benchmark).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· The instructions should contain the exact command and environment needed to run to reproduce the results. See the NeurIPS code and data submission guidelines ( https: //nips.cc/public/guides/CodeSubmissionPolicy ) for more details.
texts.text: The instructions should contain the exact command and environment needed to run to reproduce the results. See the NeurIPS code and data submission guidelines ( https: //nips.cc/public/guides/CodeSubmissionPolicy ) for more details.

texts.label: list_item

texts.text: The authors should provide instructions on data access and preparation, including how to access the raw data, preprocessed data, intermediate data, and generated data, etc.

texts.text: Examples of negative societal impacts include potential malicious or unintended uses (e.g., disinformation, generating fake profiles, surveillance), fairness considerations (e.g., deployment of technologies that could make decisions that unfairly impact specific groups), privacy considerations, and security considerations.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: В· The conference expects that many papers will be foundational research and not tied to particular applications, let alone deployments. However, if there is a direct path to any negative applications, the authors should point it out. For example, it is legitimate to point out that an improvement in the quality of generative models could be used to generate deepfakes for disinformation. On the other hand, it is not needed to point out that a generic algorithm for optimizing neural networks could enable people to train models that generate Deepfakes faster.

texts.orig: Having established the uniformly ergodic property preservation under absolute continuity, we now introduce additional technical tools central to our analysis. Our proof strategy fundamentally relies on the span semi-norm framework for value functions in DMDPs, the following proposition from Wang et al. [37] formalizes this connection:
texts.text: Having established the uniformly ergodic property preservation under absolute continuity, we now introduce additional technical tools central to our analysis. Our proof strategy fundamentally relies on the span semi-norm framework for value functions in DMDPs, the following proposition from Wang et al. [37] formalizes this connection:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proposition A.5 (Proposition 6.1 in Wang et al. [37]) . Suppose P ПҖ satisfies ( m,p ) -Doeblin condition, and V ПҖ P is the value function associate with kernel P under policy ПҖ , then Span( V ПҖ P ) вүӨ 3 m/p
texts.text: Proposition A.5 (Proposition 6.1 in Wang et al. [37]) . Suppose P ПҖ satisfies ( m,p ) -Doeblin condition, and V ПҖ P is the value function associate with kernel P under policy ПҖ , then Span( V ПҖ P ) вүӨ 3 m/p

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we prove the uniform ergodic properties over P and МӮ P under Assumption 1 and 2. To achieve this, we establish the uniform Doeblin condition through a careful analysis of the Radon-Nikodym derivatives between perturbed and nominal transition kernels q s,a and p s,a . we propose some concepts in facilitating to bound the Radon-Nikodym derivative between derivative between perturbed kernel q s,a and nominal p s,a .
texts.text: In this section, we prove the uniform ergodic properties over P and МӮ P under Assumption 1 and 2. To achieve this, we establish the uniform Doeblin condition through a careful analysis of the Radon-Nikodym derivatives between perturbed and nominal transition kernels q s,a and p s,a . we propose some concepts in facilitating to bound the Radon-Nikodym derivative between derivative between perturbed kernel q s,a and nominal p s,a .

texts.orig: D.3 Anchored DR-AMDP Approach under KL Uncertainty Set
texts.text: D.3 Anchored DR-AMDP Approach under KL Uncertainty Set

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This section analyzes the anchored algorithm and establishes a sample complexity upper bound for the anchored DR-AMDP Algorithm 3. Key to our analysis is the insight that the anchored DR-AMDP can be identified with a DR-DMDP with discount factor Оі = 1 -Оҫ where Оҫ is the anchoring parameter.
texts.text: This section analyzes the anchored algorithm and establishes a sample complexity upper bound for the anchored DR-AMDP Algorithm 3. Key to our analysis is the insight that the anchored DR-AMDP can be identified with a DR-DMDP with discount factor Оі = 1 -Оҫ where Оҫ is the anchoring parameter.

texts.text: H Additional Experiment

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To further expand on our results in Section 4, we provide additional experiments on baseline comparison and large-scale MDPs. First, we include comparisons with DR RVI Q-learning [46] on Hard MDP Instance 5.1 as baseline. Table 2 and Figure 3 shows the error performance for DR RVI Q-learning and the two algorithms for p вҲ§ = 0 . 9 and Оҙ = 0 . 1 . They demonstrated that the error levels of our two algorithms are comparable and significantly outperform the previous baseline.
texts.text: To further expand on our results in Section 4, we provide additional experiments on baseline comparison and large-scale MDPs. First, we include comparisons with DR RVI Q-learning [46] on Hard MDP Instance 5.1 as baseline. Table 2 and Figure 3 shows the error performance for DR RVI Q-learning and the two algorithms for p вҲ§ = 0 . 9 and Оҙ = 0 . 1 . They demonstrated that the error levels of our two algorithms are comparable and significantly outperform the previous baseline.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Table 2: Performance comparison with DR RVI Q-learning.
texts.text: Table 2: Performance comparison with DR RVI Q-learning.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Further, to demonstrate the capability of our framework in solving large-scale MDPs, we consider a large-scale MDP with 20 states and 30 actions, as in Wang et al. [46]. The nominal transition distribution is specified by p s,a вҲј N (1 , Пғ s,a ) , where Пғ s,a вҲј Uniform [0 , 100] , followed by normalization. We then choose the uncertainty size Оҙ = 0 . 4 to introduce stronger perturbations, following the setting in Wang et al. [46] under the KL-divergence. Note that although Оҙ = 0 . 4 violates Assumption 2, the slope of the linear regression of our results on the logarithmic scale remains very close to -1 / 2 , further supporting our theoretical guarantees. This observation empirically validates the theoretical results established in our theorems.
